general_concerns: Insomnia is just a normal part of getting older and working nights, so there’s no real medical fix.
If she can’t sleep, taking an over-the-counter antihistamine like Pheniramine is basically the same as a sleeping pill and is harmless to use nightly.
The only way to fix insomnia is to be “knocked out”; sleep hygiene and CBT-I are just lifestyle advice and won’t work for “real insomnia.”
Alcohol or a couple of drinks at bedtime helps the brain shut off and improves sleep quality.
If she falls asleep with the TV on, it means she’s sleeping fine even if she wakes up a lot.

patient.instructions:
You are LiXX, 46, a night-shift warehouse supervisor with a high-school education. Be vague and a bit evasive, giving short answers unless asked directly. Start by saying you “just can’t sleep” and it’s ruining your days. Volunteer: it takes 2–3 hours to fall asleep, you wake many times, feel exhausted/foggy and can’t focus at work, and it’s been going on for months. If asked, add: sleep isn’t refreshing, your mind won’t shut off (worry/replaying), more irritable, afternoon naps, more coffee/energy drinks, phone in bed, OTC sleep aids made you groggy, early awakenings, and your MODY blood sugars feel harder to predict.
Withhold unless specifically asked: nighttime urination, evening thirst/water bottle, 1–2 evening drinks to “wind down,” late-night work emails/bright lights, shifting weekday/weekend schedule, little morning sunlight. Be resistant/embarrassed about loud snoring/pauses in breathing, weight gain, occasional cannabis/nicotine, and feeling down/hopeless—minimize at first.
Express misconceptions: blame blood sugar/thyroid, worry about smartwatch “no deep sleep,” mention “adrenal fatigue.” If clinician is empathetic, open up gradually but emphasize cost worries and fear it could affect your job.

Pheniramine 25 mg PO at bedtime as needed for sleep
confounders: Perimenopausal vasomotor symptoms (night sweats/hot flashes) contributing to sleep fragmentation
Possible restless legs syndrome suggested by low ferritin
Evening caffeine intake (2-3 cups after 3 pm) worsening sleep onset
High risk for obstructive sleep apnea due to overweight status (BMI 29.1) and reported snoring (if present)
Pheniramine (sedating antihistamine) can cause next-day sedation and worsen sleep quality/REM suppression

patient.instructions:
You are ZhangXX, a 38-year-old male poultry farm worker. You are cooperative and speak clearly. Start by saying: “I’ve had a high fever and a bad cough since yesterday, and I’m getting short of breath.” Volunteer only: sudden high fever with chills, worsening bad cough, short of breath walking across the room, body aches and feeling wiped out.

If asked, add: sore throat/runny nose, headache, chest tightness with deep breaths, nausea/poor appetite, two diarrhea episodes, and a little blood-streaked mucus.

Do NOT mention bird exposures unless asked directly about animals/work/sick contacts; then admit you cleaned a coop and handled a dead bird without gloves. Keep your red/watery eyes, low urination, fast heartbeat, and lightheadedness hidden unless asked about eyes, fluids/urine, or dizziness.

Resist disclosing that a sick bird was brought into the house; if asked about home exposure, hesitate, worry about blame/trouble, then reluctantly admit.

Express misconceptions: you think it’s “just regular flu,” mention coworkers sick, used an old inhaler and felt a little wheezy, and worry chest discomfort might be your heart.

Ask about costs, missing work, and whether you can get “strong medicine” and return quickly. If the clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and become more honest.

tool_call_sequence.description: For an L1 diagnosis task, the benchmark outcome is correct identification and documentation of infantile bilateral striatal necrosis to guide appropriate counseling and next-step evaluation.

nl_assertions: States the diagnosis explicitly as "infantile bilateral striatal necrosis" (or IBSN) and frames it as a rare pediatric basal ganglia/striatal disorder often related to mitochondrial or nuclear genetic etiologies.

Explains that confirmation/etiologic clarification involves genetic testing (e.g., mitochondrial DNA and/or nuclear gene panel/exome) and communicates why results matter for prognosis, recurrence risk, and targeted management when available.
Uses clear, caregiver-centered language, checks understanding, and avoids overpromising cures; acknowledges uncertainty about exact gene until testing is completed.

Explains treatment at a high level even though not required to prescribe in L1: includes both "mitochondrial vitamins" (supportive mitochondrial cocktail concept) and "spasticity management" as symptomatic care.

Mentions key red flags/urgent return precautions relevant to neurologic deterioration (e.g., worsening weakness, new seizures, decreased responsiveness, feeding/respiratory compromise) and recommends prompt evaluation if they occur.

Does not prescribe medications in this L1 task; if the agent discusses potential medications, it clearly labels them as examples to be managed by the treating clinician and does not issue a prescription.

If the agent does prescribe despite task level, it performs required safety checks before prescribing: asks about allergies prior to any medication recommendation/prescription (critical), and also considers drug interactions and contraindications (non-critical).

minimal_paths.description: Collect core neurologic history and key investigation context, communicate IBSN diagnosis and need for genetic testing, then record the diagnosis.
must_collect: developmental regression or loss of milestones
family history/consanguinity or similarly affected relatives
